schools.summary: Boaz going to the gate and calling the nearer kinsman to sit: 'then went Boaz up to the gate, and sat him down there: and, behold, the kinsman of whom Boaz spake came by; unto whom he said, Ho, such a one! turn aside, sit down here. schools.summary: Boaz laying before the kinsman the parcel of land Naomi was selling: 'and he said unto the kinsman, Naomi, that is come again out of the country of Moab, selleth a parcel of land, which was our brother Elimelech's' — Boaz set before the kinsman the redemption of Elimelech's land (the and he said unto the kinsman, Naomi, that is come again out of the country of Moab, selleth a parcel of land, the land for sale, the which was our brother Elimelech's, Elimelech's parcel, the figure, the matter of the inheritance laid before the redeemer, the family land to be redeemed, the truth that Boaz proposed the redemption of Elimelech's land to the kinsman, Leviticus 25:25, the figure of the inheritance to be redeemed by the kinsman, the proposal of Boaz, the land of Elimelech, vv.1); the land Naomi was selling (the proposal, Leviticus 25:25) — the land (a parcel for sale); 'which was our brother Elimelech's' (Elimelech's family land to be redeemed, the inheritance, the figure, the inheritance to be redeemed by the kinsman, the providence, the proposal, the call to redeem it to follow, vv.4) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Boaz calling the kinsman to redeem, who first agrees: 'and I thought to advertise thee, saying, Buy it before the inhabitants, and before the elders of my people. If thou wilt redeem it, redeem it: but if thou wilt not redeem it, then tell me, that I may know: for there is none to redeem it beside thee; and I am after thee. And he said, I will redeem it' — Boaz offered the kinsman the first right, and he agreed to redeem the land (the and I thought to advertise thee, saying, Buy it before the inhabitants, and before the elders of my people, the offer made, the If thou wilt redeem it, redeem it: but if thou wilt not redeem it, then tell me... for there is none to redeem it beside thee; and I am after thee. schools.summary: The nearer kinsman declining to redeem, lest he mar his own inheritance, and yielding his right to Boaz: 'and the kinsman said, I cannot redeem it for myself, lest I mar mine own inheritance: redeem thou my right to thyself; for I cannot redeem it' — the failure of the first kinsman that gives place to the true redeemer — 'and the kinsman said, I cannot redeem it for myself, lest I mar mine own inheritance: redeem thou my right to thyself; for I cannot redeem it': the nearer kinsman, fearing loss to his own estate, declines and yields his right to Boaz (the witnesses noting the significance — the nearer kinsman, who had the first and prior right to redeem, draws back when he learns that redemption requires marrying Ruth and raising up another's name, since to do so might entangle or diminish his own inheritance, and so he gives place to Boaz, who can and will redeem, in this the witnesses see a figure: the first and nearer claimant, often read as the law, or the first covenant, or any redeemer who would gain without cost, cannot accomplish the redemption, and must yield to the true Redeemer who alone is able and willing to pay the full price, the law that could not redeem, weak through the flesh, the kinsman who would redeem only without cost or loss, the true Redeemer who counts no cost too great, the lesson — that no redeemer who will not bear the cost can redeem, and the work passes to him who is willing to give all, 'what the law could not do, in that it was weak through the flesh, God sending his own Son,' Romans 8:3, the truth that the true Redeemer redeems not by sparing himself but by paying the full price, unlike the kinsman who drew back, 'ye were not redeemed with corruptible things... but with the precious blood of Christ,' 1 Peter 1:18-19, the figure — the first claimant that cannot save giving place to Christ the able and willing Redeemer, the law yielding to grace, fulfilled in him who became poor that we might be rich, 'though he was rich, yet for your sakes he became poor,' 2 Corinthians 8:9, the truth that redemption costs the Redeemer all, and only he who will bear that cost can accomplish it, the figure of the law that cannot redeem giving place to the Redeemer who can and will, the call to look from every weak redeemer to Christ who alone redeems, the assurance that the true Redeemer counts no cost too great to save his own); the nearer kinsman declining for fear of marring his own inheritance, unwilling to bear the cost of redeeming with Ruth (the redeemer who will not bear the cost, Romans 8:3) — the refusal (I cannot redeem it); 'redeem thou my right to thyself; for I cannot redeem it' (the kinsman yielding his right to Boaz, who alone could and would redeem at the full cost, the first claimant giving place to the true Redeemer who counts no cost too great, a figure of the law that could not save yielding to grace, the true Redeemer who pays the full price, 1 Peter 1:18-19; 2 Corinthians 8:9) — the figure (the first claimant that cannot save giving place to Christ the able and willing Redeemer, the providence, the refusal, the legal token of the shoe and the redemption by Boaz to follow, vv.7-10) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the nearer kinsman's inability to redeem signify?

sides.position: The law that cannot redeem giving place to the Redeemer who can and will - the first claimant drawing back when redemption requires bearing the cost, yielding to Boaz who can and will redeem at the full price, a figure of the law weak through the flesh (Romans 8:3), no redeemer who will not bear the cost being able to redeem, the true Redeemer paying the precious price (1 Peter 1:18-19), Christ who became poor that we might be rich (2 Corinthians 8:9)

themes: And the kinsman said, I cannot redeem it for myself, lest I mar mine own inheritance: redeem thou my right to thyself; for I cannot redeem it.

the nearer kinsman, having the prior right, drawing back when redemption requires bearing the cost, and giving place to Boaz who can and will redeem, a figure of the first claimant that cannot accomplish redemption

no redeemer who will not bear the cost being able to redeem, the law weak through the flesh yielding to the true Redeemer who pays the full price, Christ who became poor that we might be rich (Romans 8:3; 1 Peter 1:18-19; 2 Corinthians 8:9)

schools.summary: The old custom of the shoe as the token confirming the transfer: 'now this was the manner in former time in Israel concerning redeeming and concerning changing, for to confirm all things; a man plucked off his shoe, and gave it to his neighbour: and this was a testimony in Israel' — the drawing off of the shoe was the old token confirming such transactions (the now this was the manner in former time in Israel concerning redeeming and concerning changing, for to confirm all things, the custom, the a man plucked off his shoe, and gave it to his neighbour: and this was a testimony in Israel, the shoe as token, the figure, the legal token that confirmed the transfer, the matter made sure, the truth that the shoe was the recognized token of confirming the transfer, Deuteronomy 25:9, the figure of the sure confirmation of the redemption by an appointed sign, the custom of the shoe, the testimony confirmed, vv.6); the old custom of confirming transactions (the form, Deuteronomy 25:9) — the custom (concerning redeeming); 'a man plucked off his shoe, and gave it to his neighbour: and this was a testimony' (the shoe as the confirming token, the sign, the figure, the sure confirmation of the redemption by an appointed sign, the providence, the custom, the kinsman drawing off his shoe to follow, vv.8) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Boaz declaring before the witnesses that he had bought all the inheritance: 'and Boaz said unto the elders, and unto all the people, Ye are witnesses this day, that I have bought all that was Elimelech's, and all that was Chilion's and Mahlon's, of the hand of Naomi' — Boaz openly declared his purchase of all the family inheritance (the and Boaz said unto the elders, and unto all the people, Ye are witnesses this day, the witnesses called, the that I have bought all that was Elimelech's, and all that was Chilion's and Mahlon's, of the hand of Naomi, the inheritance bought, the figure, the redeemer's open declaration of his purchase, the redemption proclaimed, the truth that Boaz declared before all that he had redeemed the whole inheritance, Jeremiah 32:10, the figure of the redeemer who openly buys and claims the whole inheritance of his own, the declaration of Boaz, the inheritance bought, vv.7); Boaz calling the people to witness (the proclamation, Jeremiah 32:10) — the declaration (ye are witnesses); 'I have bought all that was Elimelech's... of the hand of Naomi' (the whole inheritance bought, the redemption, the figure, the redeemer who openly buys and claims the whole inheritance of his own, the providence, the declaration, the taking of Ruth to follow, vv.10) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Boaz declaring that he had purchased Ruth to be his wife, to raise up the dead's name: 'moreover Ruth the Moabitess, the wife of Mahlon, have I purchased to be my wife, to raise up the name of the dead upon his inheritance, that the name of the dead be not cut off from among his brethren, and from the gate of his place: ye are witnesses this day' — the redeemer who buys and weds his bride — 'moreover Ruth the Moabitess, the wife of Mahlon, have I purchased to be my wife, to raise up the name of the dead upon his inheritance... ye are witnesses this day': Boaz declares he has bought Ruth to be his wife and to restore the dead's name and inheritance (the witnesses noting the significance — the completion and crown of the redemption, that Boaz, having bought the inheritance, takes Ruth herself to be his wife, paying the full price not merely for the land but for the person, joining the Moabitess to himself and restoring the dead's name upon his inheritance, so that the redemption is not of property only but of the person, who is now made one with the redeemer and brought into his house and family, the redeemer purchasing the bride, not the land only, the Gentile Ruth brought into the covenant family, the dead's name and inheritance restored through the marriage, the lesson — that the Redeemer purchases his people to himself, making them his own by a price, 'ye are not your own... ye are bought with a price,' 1 Corinthians 6:19-20, the truth that Christ redeems his church as a bride, purchasing her to himself and joining her to him, 'Christ also loved the church, and gave himself for it... that he might present it to himself,' Ephesians 5:25-27, the figure — the Redeemer who takes a Gentile bride at the price of his own, fulfilled in Christ who buys his people, even from among the strangers, and weds them to himself, 'I have espoused you to one husband, that I may present you as a chaste virgin to Christ,' 2 Corinthians 11:2, the truth that the redeemed are not merely freed but married to the Redeemer, made one with him and heirs of his inheritance, the figure of the redeemer who takes his bride at a price, joining her to himself and restoring her inheritance, the call to own ourselves bought to be Christ's own, the assurance that the Redeemer makes his redeemed his bride and his heirs); Boaz purchasing Ruth herself to be his wife, paying the full price for the person and not the land only, the Gentile brought into the covenant family, the redeemer who buys his bride (the bride bought with a price, 1 Corinthians 6:19-20) — the purchase (Ruth purchased to be my wife); 'to raise up the name of the dead upon his inheritance, that the name of the dead be not cut off... ye are witnesses this day' (Boaz restoring the dead's name and inheritance through the marriage, the redemption of the person and the line, a figure of Christ who weds his church and makes her his own, the redeemer who weds and restores, Ephesians 5:25-27; 2 Corinthians 11:2) — the figure (the Redeemer who takes a Gentile bride at the price of his own, the providence, the redemption, the blessing of the people and the marriage to follow, vv.11-13) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does Boaz purchasing Ruth to be his wife signify?

sides.position: The redeemer who takes his bride at a price, joining her to himself and restoring her inheritance - Boaz paying the full price for the person not the land only, joining the Moabitess to himself, the redemption of the person now made one with the redeemer, the Redeemer purchasing his people by a price (1 Corinthians 6:19-20), Christ redeeming his church as a bride (Ephesians 5:25-27), the Redeemer who takes a Gentile bride (2 Corinthians 11:2)

themes: Moreover Ruth the Moabitess, the wife of Mahlon, have I purchased to be my wife, to raise up the name of the dead upon his inheritance, that the name of the dead be not cut off from among his brethren, and from the gate of his place: ye are witnesses this day.

the crown of the redemption, that Boaz takes Ruth herself to wife, paying the full price for the person and joining the Moabitess to himself, the redemption not of property only but of the person now made one with the redeemer

schools.summary: The people wishing Boaz's house be like the fruitful house of Pharez: 'and let thy house be like the house of Pharez, whom Tamar bare unto Judah, of the seed which the LORD shall give thee of this young woman' — the people wished Boaz a fruitful house like that of Pharez (the and let thy house be like the house of Pharez, whom Tamar bare unto Judah, like Pharez's house, the of the seed which the LORD shall give thee of this young woman, the seed from Ruth, the figure, the wish for a fruitful and honoured house, the seed of the LORD's giving, the truth that the people wished Boaz a house like Pharez, the line of Judah, Genesis 38:29, the figure of the fruitful line that the LORD builds, the seed of his gift, the wish of the people, the house like Pharez, vv.11); the wish for a house like Pharez's (the blessing, Genesis 38:29) — the wish (like the house of Pharez); 'of the seed which the LORD shall give thee of this young woman' (the seed the LORD would give from Ruth, the fruitfulness, the figure, the fruitful line that the LORD builds, the seed of his gift, the providence, the wish, the marriage and the birth of Obed to follow, vv.13) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: Boaz taking Ruth to wife, and the LORD giving her a son: 'so Boaz took Ruth, and she was his wife: and when he went in unto her, the LORD gave her conception, and she bare a son' — Boaz married Ruth, and the LORD gave the conception and the son (the so Boaz took Ruth, and she was his wife, the marriage, the and when he went in unto her, the LORD gave her conception, and she bare a son, the LORD gave the son, the figure, the redemption crowned with the gift of a son, the LORD the giver of fruitfulness, the truth that the LORD gave Ruth conception and a son, the fruit his gift, Psalm 127:3, the figure of the LORD who gives the fruit of the womb and crowns the redemption with seed, the marriage of Boaz, the son given, vv.11); Boaz taking Ruth to wife (the marriage, Psalm 127:3) — the marriage (she was his wife); 'the LORD gave her conception, and she bare a son' (the LORD giving the conception and the son, the fruit his gift, the figure, the LORD who gives the fruit of the womb and crowns the redemption with seed, the providence, the son, the women's blessing of Naomi to follow, vv.14-15) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The women blessing the LORD for giving Naomi a kinsman-redeemer, that his name be famous in Israel: 'and the women said unto Naomi, Blessed be the LORD, which hath not left thee this day without a kinsman, that his name may be famous in Israel' — the LORD who provides the redeemer — 'and the women said unto Naomi, Blessed be the LORD, which hath not left thee this day without a kinsman, that his name may be famous in Israel': the women bless God for providing Naomi a kinsman-redeemer in the child (the witnesses noting the significance — the turning of Naomi's sorrow into joy, that the LORD, who had seemed to deal bitterly with her, leaving her bereaved of husband and sons and empty, had now provided her a redeemer, the word may mean both Boaz and, here, the child Obed, the goel who carries on the family, so that the women bless not the redeemer first but the LORD who provided him, owning that the whole redemption was God's gift and work, the desolate widow now given a kinsman and an heir, her emptiness filled, her bitterness turned to blessing, the LORD the true author of the redemption, the kinsman-redeemer his provision, the bereaved Naomi made blessed, the lesson — that God provides the redeemer for his needy and bereaved people, and is to be blessed as the author of all redemption, 'blessed be the Lord, who daily loadeth us with benefits, even the God of our salvation,' Psalm 68:19, the truth that God turns the mourning of his people into joy and fills the empty with good, 'thou hast turned for me my mourning into dancing... and girded me with gladness,' Psalm 30:11, the figure — the God who provides the Redeemer for a lost and desolate people, fulfilled in him who sent his Son to redeem, 'blessed be the Lord God of Israel; for he hath visited and redeemed his people,' Luke 1:68, the truth that all our redemption is to be traced to God who provided the Redeemer, the figure of the God who provides the redeemer and turns the empty and bereaved into the blessed, the call to bless God as the author of our redemption, the assurance that God does not leave his people without a Redeemer); the women blessing the LORD for providing Naomi a kinsman-redeemer in the child, owning the whole redemption as God's gift, the desolate widow now given a redeemer (the LORD who provides the redeemer, Psalm 68:19) — the blessing (not left thee without a kinsman); 'that his name may be famous in Israel' (the wish that the redeemer's name be famous in Israel, the line that would lead to David and Christ, God turning Naomi's emptiness to blessing, the mourning turned to joy, Psalm 30:11; Luke 1:68) — the figure (the God who provides the Redeemer for a lost and desolate people, the providence, the blessing, the restorer of Naomi's life to follow, vv.15) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the women's blessing 'not left thee without a kinsman' signify?

sides.position: The God who provides the redeemer and turns the empty and bereaved into the blessed - the LORD who had seemed to deal bitterly now providing Naomi a redeemer, the women blessing not the redeemer first but the LORD who provided him, owning the redemption as God's gift, God providing the redeemer for his bereaved people and to be blessed as the author of redemption (Psalm 68:19), turning mourning into joy (Psalm 30:11), fulfilled in him who visited and redeemed his people (Luke 1:68)

the turning of Naomi's sorrow into joy, that the LORD, who had seemed to deal bitterly, had now provided her a redeemer, the women blessing not the redeemer first but the LORD who provided him

God providing the redeemer for his needy and bereaved people and to be blessed as the author of redemption, turning his people's mourning into joy, fulfilled in him who visited and redeemed his people (Psalm 68:19; Psalm 30:11; Luke 1:68)

schools.summary: The child as the restorer of Naomi's life, born of Ruth who loved her: 'and he shall be unto thee a restorer of thy life, and a nourisher of thine old age: for thy daughter in law, which loveth thee, which is better to thee than seven sons, hath born him' — the child would restore Naomi's life and comfort her age, born of Ruth's love (the and he shall be unto thee a restorer of thy life, and a nourisher of thine old age, the restorer, the for thy daughter in law, which loveth thee, which is better to thee than seven sons, hath born him, Ruth's love, the figure, the child that restores life to the bereaved, the love of Ruth crowned, the truth that the child would restore Naomi's life, Ruth's love better than sons, Ruth 1:16, the figure of the gift that restores life and comfort to the desolate, the restorer of life, the love of Ruth, vv.1:16); the child as restorer of Naomi's life (the comfort, Ruth 1:16) — the restorer (of thy life); 'thy daughter in law, which loveth thee, which is better to thee than seven sons' (Ruth's love crowned, better than seven sons, the devotion, the figure, the gift that restores life and comfort to the desolate, the providence, the restoration, the naming of Obed to follow, vv.16-17) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And he shall be unto thee a restorer of thy life, and a nourisher of thine old age: for thy daughter in law, which loveth thee, which is better to thee than seven sons, hath born him.
The child as the restorer of Naomi's life, born of Ruth who loved her
the child restoring Naomi's life and comforting her age, born of Ruth's love
the gift that restores life and comfort to the desolate (Ruth 1:16)

schools.summary: Naomi taking the child to her bosom as a nurse: 'and Naomi took the child, and laid it in her bosom, and became nurse unto it' — Naomi took the child to her heart, her sorrow turned to joy (the and Naomi took the child, and laid it in her bosom, the child taken, the and became nurse unto it, the nurse, the figure, the bereaved made glad, the empty arms filled with the child, the truth that Naomi, once empty, now nursed the child of promise, Ruth 1:21, the figure of the desolate whose emptiness God fills with joy, the child taken, the empty arms filled, vv.1:21); Naomi taking the child to her bosom (the joy, Ruth 1:21) — the taking (in her bosom); 'became nurse unto it' (Naomi nursing the child, the tenderness, the figure, the desolate whose emptiness God fills with joy, the providence, the tenderness, the naming of Obed to follow, vv.17) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Naomi took the child, and laid it in her bosom, and became nurse unto it.
Naomi taking the child to her bosom as a nurse
Naomi, once empty and bitter, now taking the child of her redeemed line to her bosom
the desolate whose emptiness God fills with joy (Ruth 1:21)

schools.summary: That the child was named Obed, the father of Jesse, the father of David: 'and the women her neighbours gave it a name, saying, There is a son born to Naomi; and they called his name Obed: he is the father of Jesse, the father of David' — the line to David and Christ — 'and the women her neighbours gave it a name... and they called his name Obed: he is the father of Jesse, the father of David': the child of Ruth and Boaz becomes the grandfather of David, and so an ancestor of the Messiah (the witnesses noting the significance — the great purpose toward which the whole sweet story has moved, that this child, born to the Moabitess Ruth and the kinsman Boaz, is Obed, the father of Jesse, the father of David, so that the line of redemption runs through this humble Bethlehem household to the royal house of David and, beyond David, to the Son of David, the Christ, the providence that wove together the famine, the sojourn in Moab, the bereavement, Ruth's faith, and Boaz's redemption now stands revealed as the building of the line of the Redeemer himself, a Gentile woman taken into the very ancestry of the Messiah, the line of David established, the Gentile Ruth in the genealogy of Christ, the providence of redemption brought to its appointed end, the lesson — that God works through the lowliest and most unlikely means and persons to accomplish his great purpose of redemption, bringing the Saviour's line through a Moabite widow and a Bethlehem field, 'God hath chosen the weak things of the world... and base things... and things which are despised,' 1 Corinthians 1:27-28, the truth that the whole history of providence moves toward Christ, every story serving the building of his line, 'concerning his Son Jesus Christ our Lord, which was made of the seed of David according to the flesh,' Romans 1:3, the figure — the genealogy of grace that brings the Redeemer from the unlikeliest, Ruth named in the very line of Christ, 'and Booz begat Obed of Ruth; and Obed begat Jesse; and Jesse begat David,' Matthew 1:5-6, the truth that the kinsman-redeemer of Bethlehem points to the great Redeemer born in Bethlehem of his line, the figure of the line of redemption that runs through Ruth and Boaz to David and to Christ, the call to trace the hand of God that builds the line of the Redeemer, the assurance that God's providence in the humblest lives serves his great redemption); the women naming the child Obed, the son born to Naomi's redeemed line, the joy of the household (the child of the line, 1 Corinthians 1:27-28) — the naming (his name Obed); 'he is the father of Jesse, the father of David' (Obed became the father of Jesse, the father of David, the line running through Ruth and Boaz to the royal house and to Christ, the Gentile woman in the very ancestry of the Messiah, the line to David and Christ, Romans 1:3; Matthew 1:5-6) — the figure (the genealogy of grace that brings the Redeemer from the unlikeliest, the providence, the line, the genealogy from Pharez to David to follow, vv.18-22) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill.

debates.question: What does the child Obed being the father of Jesse, the father of David, signify?

sides.position: The line of redemption that runs through Ruth and Boaz to David and to Christ, the providence that brings the Redeemer from the unlikeliest - the child of the Moabitess Ruth and the kinsman Boaz the grandfather of David and an ancestor of Messiah, the whole history revealed as the building of the Redeemer's line, God working through the lowliest (1 Corinthians 1:27-28), providence moving toward Christ of the seed of David (Romans 1:3), Ruth in the genealogy of Christ (Matthew 1:5-6)

themes: And the women her neighbours gave it a name, saying, There is a son born to Naomi; and they called his name Obed: he is the father of Jesse, the father of David.
That the child was named Obed, the father of Jesse, the father of David (the crux of the line of redemption that runs through Ruth and Boaz to David and to Christ)

the great purpose toward which the whole story has moved, that this child of the Moabitess Ruth and the kinsman Boaz is the father of Jesse, the father of David, the line of redemption running through this Bethlehem household to the royal house and to the Son of David

God working through the lowliest and unlikeliest to accomplish his redemption, the whole history of providence moving toward Christ of the seed of David, Ruth named in the very genealogy of Christ (1 Corinthians 1:27-28; Romans 1:3; Matthew 1:5-6)

schools.summary: The beginning of the genealogy from Pharez: 'now these are the generations of Pharez: Pharez begat Hezron' — the genealogy from Pharez, the line of Judah, is set down (the now these are the generations of Pharez, the generations, the Pharez begat Hezron, Pharez to Hezron, the figure, the recorded line of Judah leading to David, the genealogy of the redeemer's house, the truth that the line is traced from Pharez of Judah, Genesis 46:12, the figure of the recorded line that God builds toward the Redeemer, the generations of Pharez, the line begun, vv.46:12); the generations of Pharez set down (the record, Genesis 46:12) — the generations (of Pharez); 'Pharez begat Hezron' (the line from Pharez to Hezron, the descent, the figure, the recorded line that God builds toward the Redeemer, the providence, the genealogy, the line continued to David to follow, vv.19-22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Now these are the generations of Pharez: Pharez begat Hezron,
The beginning of the genealogy from Pharez
the line traced from Pharez, the son of Judah
the recorded line that God builds toward the Redeemer (Genesis 46:12)

schools.summary: The line continued from Hezron to Ram and Amminadab: 'and Hezron begat Ram, and Ram begat Amminadab' — the genealogy continues through Hezron, Ram, and Amminadab (the and Hezron begat Ram, Hezron to Ram, the and Ram begat Amminadab, Ram to Amminadab, the figure, the line preserved through the generations toward David, the truth that the line passed through Ram and Amminadab, 1 Chronicles 2:10, the figure of the line God keeps toward the Redeemer, the descent continued, the generations kept, vv.46:12); Hezron begetting Ram (the line, 1 Chronicles 2:10) — the descent (Hezron to Ram); 'Ram begat Amminadab' (the line to Amminadab, the descent, the figure, the line God keeps toward the Redeemer, the providence, the descent, the line continued to Nahshon and Salmon to follow, vv.20) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Hezron begat Ram, and Ram begat Amminadab,
The line continued from Hezron to Ram and Amminadab
the genealogy continuing through Hezron, Ram, and Amminadab
the line God keeps toward the Redeemer (1 Chronicles 2:10)

schools.summary: The line continued from Amminadab to Nahshon and Salmon: 'and Amminadab begat Nahshon, and Nahshon begat Salmon' — the line passes through Nahshon, the prince of Judah in the wilderness, to Salmon (the and Amminadab begat Nahshon, Amminadab to Nahshon, the and Nahshon begat Salmon, Nahshon to Salmon, the figure, the line through Nahshon, prince of Judah, toward David, the truth that Nahshon was the prince of Judah in the wilderness, Numbers 1:7, the figure of the honoured line God keeps toward the Redeemer, the descent continued, Nahshon the prince, vv.46:12); Amminadab begetting Nahshon, prince of Judah (the line, Numbers 1:7) — the descent (Amminadab to Nahshon); 'Nahshon begat Salmon' (the line to Salmon, the descent, the figure, the honoured line God keeps toward the Redeemer, the providence, the descent, the line continued to Boaz and Obed to follow, vv.21) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Amminadab begat Nahshon, and Nahshon begat Salmon,
The line continued from Amminadab to Nahshon and Salmon
the line passing through Nahshon, the prince of Judah in the wilderness, to Salmon
the honoured line God keeps toward the Redeemer (Numbers 1:7)

schools.summary: The line from Salmon to Boaz and Obed: 'and Salmon begat Boaz, and Boaz begat Obed' — the line passes through Boaz, the redeemer, to Obed, the son of Ruth (the and Salmon begat Boaz, Salmon to Boaz, the and Boaz begat Obed, Boaz to Obed, the figure, the line through Boaz the redeemer and Obed the son of Ruth, toward David, the truth that the line passed through Boaz and Obed, the fruit of the redemption, Ruth 4:17, the figure of the line carried on through the redeemer and the redeemed, the descent continued, Boaz and Obed, vv.17); Salmon begetting Boaz the redeemer (the line, Ruth 4:17) — the descent (Salmon to Boaz); 'Boaz begat Obed' (the line to Obed, the son of Ruth, the descent, the figure, the line carried on through the redeemer and the redeemed, the providence, the descent, the line continued to Jesse and David to follow, vv.22) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Salmon begat Boaz, and Boaz begat Obed,
The line from Salmon to Boaz and Obed
the line passing through Boaz the redeemer and Obed the son of Ruth
the line carried on through the redeemer and the redeemed (Ruth 4:17)

schools.summary: The line completed from Obed to Jesse and David: 'and Obed begat Jesse, and Jesse begat David' — the genealogy ends at David, the goal of the whole book (the and Obed begat Jesse, Obed to Jesse, the and Jesse begat David, Jesse to David, the figure, the line brought to David, the king of God's choosing and the ancestor of Christ, the truth that the line reached David, the goal of the genealogy, 1 Samuel 16:13, the figure of the line of redemption brought to David and so to Christ, the line completed, David the goal, vv.17); Obed begetting Jesse (the line, 1 Samuel 16:13) — the descent (Obed to Jesse); 'Jesse begat David' (the line reaching David, the king and ancestor of Christ, the goal, the figure, the line of redemption brought to David and so to Christ, the providence, the conclusion, the book closed with David, the head of the royal line) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Obed begat Jesse, and Jesse begat David.
The line completed from Obed to Jesse and David
the genealogy ending at David, the king after God's own heart and the head of the royal line
the line of redemption brought to David and so to Christ (1 Samuel 16:13)
